MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION
Off Calendar Oct-13-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT TIMOTHY CASLMON, GHC OF SAN FRAN 180, LLC DBA CITY VIEW POST ACUTE'S MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants' motion to compel arbitration is denied.

Under California law, persons of unsound mind are not capable of contracting. (Civ. Code sec. 1556.) Whether a person has an "unsound mind" is related to the concept of consent because the parties' consent to the contract must be free. (Civ. Code, sec. 1565.) The burden of persuasion is always on the moving party to prove the existence of an arbitration agreement with the opposing party by a preponderance of the evidence: "Because the existence of the agreement is a statutory prerequisite to granting the [motion or] petition, the [party seeking arbitration] bears the burden of proving its existence by a preponderance of the evidence." (Rosenthal v. Great Western Fin. Securities Corp. (1996) 14 Cal.4th 394, 413.)

Here, Plaintiff has provided expert testimony (Gross Decl., 2-3) that "the mental conditions of Decedent Matias, as indicated in his medical records and declarations, are significant in terms of evidencing a lack of capacity to contract" and were severe enough that "he did not understand what he signed, and could not have understood, when he signed the arbitration agreement on the date of execution." The expert testimony further states that under Probate Code 811 (the factors which determine whether a person lacks the capacity to make a decision (incapacity to contract)), Decedent Matias' schizoaffective disorder and the symptoms he experienced caused "substantial impairment in thought processes and will cause severely disorganized thinking, hallucinations, delusions, and uncontrollable thoughts." (Id. at 3.) In addition, Plaintiff provided a declaration from Decedent Matias' sister (Delaney Decl., 2) stating she had the legal power of attorney to make decisions for him and she provided medical records demonstrating her advocating for Decedent Matias, as he was labeled a "poor historian" by doctors. (Delaney Decl, Ex.2.)

Defendants contend that Plaintiff failed to refute that Decedent Matias had the capacity to contract on 5/31/13, as the hospital medical records only show his condition until 5/28/13. (Reply 5-6.) Defendants rely on the medical records provided to indicate that Decedent Matias' condition was due to a temporary change in condition from not taking his medications. (Id.) However, Defendants fail to meet their burden, to prove by the preponderance of the evidence, that Decedent Matias had the capacity to contract on 5/31/13. Notably, Defendants do not address Decedent Matias' sister having power of attorney over him, or refute that his schizoaffective disorder affected his thought processes under Probate Code 811, or that he had functionally declined severely enough that he was admitted into a skilled nursing facility instead of returning home. Decedent Matias was not capable of contracting on 5/31/13, could not consent under Civ. Code, sec. 1565, and thus no arbitration agreement exists.

Order signed (single order as to both motions).

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/RBU)
